--- SOURCE 2: Progress Cleaning the Air and Improving People's Health ---
URL: https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health

SUMMARY:
Airborne lead pollution, a widespread health concern before EPA phased out lead in motor vehicle gasoline under Clean Air Act authority, now meets national air quality standards in most areas of the country. In 2016, EPA finalized findings that GHG emissions from certain classes of engines used in aircraft contribute to the air pollution that causes climate change endangering public health and welfare under section 231(a) of the Clean Air Act. Learn more about Regulations for Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Aircraft. Further reductions in power plant pollution have been achieved by state and EPA efforts to cut interstate air pollution, achieving additional public health benefits and helping downwind states meet health-based air quality standards for fine particles and ozone.

Progress Cleaning the Air and Improving People's Health
=======================================================

> The Clean Air Act has a proven record of public health and environmental protection since 1970.

Pollution reductions from EPA Partnership Programs

EPA uses voluntary partnership programs in tandem with regulatory programs to protect public health and the environment. Clean Air Act partnership programs reduce conventional air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions, improve energy efficiency, reduce oil imports, and save money. [_<Learn more about voluntary partnerships\>_](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-voluntary-partnership-program-accomplishments)

More information on the national progress toward clean air
----------------------------------------------------------

*   [Air Quality Trends](https://www.epa.gov/air-trends)
    

*   [Progress Reports on Market-Based Air Programs for Power Plants and Industry](https://www.epa.gov/power-sector/programs-progress)
    
*   [Impact of Five Major Rules for Vehicles and Engines](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-five-important-clean-air-rules-2030-vehicles-and-engines)
    
*   [History of Reducing Air Pollution from Transportation in the United States](https://www.epa.gov/transportation-air-pollution-and-climate-change/accomplishments-and-successes-reducing-air)

Quick Overview
--------------

*   [For more than forty-five years the Clean Air Act has cut pollution as the U.S. economy has grown.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#pollution)
    

*   [Americans breathe less pollution and face lower risks of premature death and other serious health effects.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#breathe)
    
*   [Environmental damage from air pollution is reduced.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#enviro)
    
*   [The value of Clean Air Act health benefits far exceeds the costs of reducing pollution.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#cost)
    
*   [New cars, trucks, and nonroad engines use state-of-the-art emission control technologies.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#cars)
    
*   [New plants and factories install modern pollution control technology.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#plants)
    

*   [Power plants have cut emissions that cause acid rain and harm public health.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#emissions)
    
*   [Interstate air pollution has been reduced.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#interstate)
    
*   [Mobile and industrial pollution sources release much less toxic pollution to the air than in 1990.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#toxic)
    
*   [Actions to protect the ozone layer are saving millions of people from skin cancers and cataracts.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#cataracts)
    
*   [The scenic vistas in our national parks are clearer due to reductions in pollution-caused haze.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#vistas)
    
*   [EPA has taken initial steps to limit emissions that cause climate change and ocean acidification.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#vistas)
    
*   [The Act has prompted deployment of clean technologies, and has helped provide impetus for technology innovations that reduce emissions and control costs.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#The%20Act%20has%20prompted%20deployment%20of%20clean%20technologies,%20and%20has%20helped%20provide%20impetus%20for%20technology%20innovations%20that%20reduce%20emissions%20and%20control%20costs)
    

* * *

Detailed Summary: Clean Air Act Results
---------------------------------------

### For more than forty-five years the Clean Air Act has cut pollution as the U.S. economy has grown.

*   Experience with the Clean Air Act since 1970 has shown that protecting public health and building the economy can go hand in hand.
    
*   Clean Air Act programs have lowered levels of six common pollutants -- particles, ozone, lead, carbon monoxide, nitrogen dioxide and sulfur dioxide -- as well as numerous toxic pollutants.
    
*   Between 1970 and 2020, the combined emissions of the six common pollutants (PM2.5 and PM10, SO2, NOx, VOCs, CO and Pb) dropped by 78 percent. This progress occurred while U.S. economic indicators remain strong.
    
*   The emissions reductions have led to dramatic improvements in the quality of the air that we breathe. Between 1990 and 2020, national concentrations of air pollutants improved 73 percent for carbon monoxide, 86 percent for lead (from 2010), 61 percent for annual nitrogen dioxide, 25 percent for ozone, 26 percent for 24-hour coarse particle concentrations, 41 percent for annual fine particles (from 2000), and 91 percent for sulfur dioxide. (For more trends information, see EPA's [Air Trends](https://www.epa.gov/air-trends) site.)
    
*   These air quality improvements have enabled many areas of the country to meet national air quality standards set to protect public health and the environment. For example, all of the 41 areas that had unhealthy levels of carbon monoxide in 1991 now have levels that meet the health-based national air quality standard. A key reason is that the motor vehicle fleet is much cleaner because of Clean Air Act emissions standards for new motor vehicles.
    
*   Airborne lead pollution, a widespread health concern before EPA phased out lead in motor vehicle gasoline under Clean Air Act authority, now meets national air quality standards in most areas of the country.
    
*   State emission control measures to implement the Act, as well as EPA's national emissions standards, have contributed to air quality improvements.
    

### Because of the Act, Americans breathe less pollution and face lower risks of premature death and other serious health effects.

A [peer-reviewed EPA study](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-clean-air-act-1990-2020-second-prospective-study) issued in March 2011 found that the Clean Air Act Amendments of 1990 are achieving large health benefits that will grow further over time as programs take full effect.

This chart shows the health benefits of Clean Air Act programs that reduce levels of fine particles and ozone.

| Health Effect Reductions (PM2.5 & Ozone Only) | Pollutant(s) | Year 2010 | Year 2020 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PM2.5 Adult Mortality | PM | 160,000 | 230,000 |
| PM2.5 Infant Mortality | PM | 230 | 280 |
| Ozone Mortality | Ozone | 4,300 | 7,100 |
| Chronic Bronchitis | PM | 54,000 | 75,000 |
| Acute Bronchitis | PM | 130,000 | 180,000 |
| Acute Myocardial Infarction | PM | 130,000 | 200,000 |
| Asthma Exacerbation | PM | 1,700,000 | 2,400,000 |
| Hospital Admissions | PM, Ozone | 86,000 | 135,000 |
| Emergency Room Visits | PM, Ozone | 86,000 | 120,000 |
| Restricted Activity Days | PM, Ozone | 84,000,000 | 110,000,000 |
| School Loss Days | Ozone | 3,200,000 | 5,400,000 |
| Lost Work Days | PM | 13,000,000 | 17,000,000 |

*   The 2011 report did not include the large benefits of the pre-1990 Clean Air Act. A peer-reviewed [1997 EPA Report to Congress](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-clean-air-act-1970-1990-study-design-and-summary-results) reviewed the benefits of the Act from 1970 to 1990, and concluded that in 1990 alone, pollution reductions under the Act prevented 205,000 early deaths, 10.4 million lost I.Q. points in children due to lead exposure, and millions of other cases of health effects.
*   Independent scientific research shows that reductions in air pollution are associated with widespread public health benefits. For example, one study found that reductions in fine particle pollution between 1980 and 2000 in U.S. cities led to improvements in average life expectancy at birth of approximately seven months.[1](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#edn1)

### Environmental damage from air pollution is reduced.

*   Lower air pollution levels mean less damage to the health of ecosystems.
    
*   Environmental effects of air pollution include damage to plants and long-term forest health, soil nutrient deterioration, accumulation of toxics in the food chain, damage to fish and other aquatic life in lakes and streams, and nitrogen enrichment of coastal estuaries causing oxygen depletion and resulting harm to fish and other aquatic animal populations.
    
*   Reducing air pollution also improves crop and timber yields, a benefit worth an estimated $5.5 billion to those industries' welfare in 2010, according to the peer-reviewed [March 2011 EPA study](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-clean-air-act-1990-2020-second-prospective-study).  Better visibility conditions in 2010 from improved air quality in selected national parks and metropolitan areas had an estimated value of $34 billion.
    

### The value of Clean Air Act health benefits far exceeds the costs of reducing pollution.

*   [EPA’s peer-reviewed 2011 study](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-clean-air-act-1990-2020-second-prospective-study) found that clean air programs established by the 1990 CAA amendments are expected to yield direct benefits to the American people which vastly exceed compliance costs.
    
*   The study's central benefits estimate of $2 trillion in 2020 exceeds costs by a factor of more than 30-to-1, and the high benefits estimate exceeds costs by 90 times. Even the low benefits estimate exceeds costs by about 3-to-1.
    
*   In addition to direct benefits vastly exceeding direct costs, economy-wide modeling conducted for the study found that the economic welfare of American households is better with post-1990 clean air programs than without them. 
    
*   Economic welfare and economic growth rates are improved because cleaner air means fewer air-pollution-related illnesses, which in turn means less money spent on medical treatments and lower absenteeism among American workers. The study projects that the beneficial economic effects of these two improvements alone more than offset the expenditures for pollution control.
    
*   The EPA report received extensive review and input from the Council on Clean Air Compliance Analysis, an independent panel of distinguished economists, scientists and public health experts established by Congress in 1991.
    

### New cars, trucks, and nonroad engines use state-of-the-art emission control technologies.

EPA has required dramatic reductions in emissions from [new motor vehicles](https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/regulations-onroad-vehicles-and-engines) and [non-road engines](https://www.epa.gov/emission-standards-reference-guide/epa-emission-standards-nonroad-engines-and-vehicles) - such as those used in construction, agriculture, industry, trains and marine vessels -- through standards that require a combination of cleaner engine technologies and cleaner fuels. In 2013, EPA estimated the benefits of [five key standards to cut emissions from vehicles, engines and fuel](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-five-important-clean-air-rules-2030-vehicles-and-engines) to 2030.

![Image 4: graph highlighting decreased voc emissions to annual vehicle miles traveled](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2015-06/vehicles.png)

*   Compared to 1970 vehicle models, new cars, SUVs and pickup trucks are roughly 99 percent cleaner for common pollutants (hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides and particle emissions), while Annual Vehicle Miles Traveled has dramatically increased.
    
*   New heavy-duty trucks and buses are roughly 99 percent cleaner than 1970 models. In August 2016, EPA and the U.S. Department of Transportation’s National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) jointly finalized standards for medium- and heavy-duty vehicles that will improve fuel efficiency and cut carbon pollution, while bolstering energy security and spurring manufacturing innovation.
    
*   Starting in the 2014 model year,  locomotives are 90 percent cleaner than pre-regulation locomotives. In March 2008, EPA finalized a three part program that dramatically reduces emissions from diesel locomotives of all types -- line-haul, switch, and passenger rail. The rule cuts particulate emissions from these engines by as much as 90 percent and nitrogen oxides emissions by as much as 80 percent when fully implemented. _[<Learn more about regulations for emissions from locomotives.\>](https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/regulations-emissions-locomotives)_
    
*   New commercial marine vessels (non-ocean-going) are 90 percent cleaner for particle emissions than in 1970. Clean Air Act and international standards for ocean-going vessel emissions and fuels are reducing emissions from ocean-going vessels as well. _[<Learn more about ocean vessels and large ships.\>](https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/domestic-regulations-emissions-marine-compression)_
    
*   EPA is taking action to reduce emissions caused by Aircraft. In 2016, EPA finalized findings that GHG emissions from certain classes of engines used in aircraft contribute to the air pollution that causes climate change endangering public health and welfare under section 231(a) of the Clean Air Act. [_Learn more about Regulations for Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Aircraft_](https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/regulations-greenhouse-gas-emissions-aircraft).
    
*   Sulfur in gasoline has been reduced by 90 percent, and sulfur in diesel fuel has been reduced by 99 percent, from pre-regulation levels.
    

### New power plants and factories use modern pollution control technology.

*   The Act requires that when new industrial facilities are designed and built, good pollution control must be part of the design. This means that as new, cleaner facilities are built, the country's industrial base becomes cleaner overall. Public health is protected as economic growth proceeds. _[<Learn more about stationary sources of air pollution.\>](https://www.epa.gov/stationary-sources-air-pollution)_
    *   In areas not meeting air quality standards, to avoid making pollution worse, new and modified large plants and factories must meet the lowest achievable emission rate and obtain offsetting emissions reductions from other sources.
    *   In areas that meet air quality standards, new and modified large plants and factories must apply the best available technology considering cost and avoid causing significant degradation of air quality or visibility impairment in national parks.
    *   For example, new coal-fired power plants typically install control devices that capture up to 98 percent of the sulfur dioxide and in many cases 90 percent of the nitrogen oxide emissions, relative to uncontrolled levels.
*   These requirements are applied through pre-construction permitting programs that are administered by state, local, tribal, or EPA permitting authorities, depending on the location.
*   State and local permitting authorities usually administer the pre-construction permit programs that determine how to apply these requirements to facilities.

### Power plants have cut emissions that cause acid rain and harm public health.

![Image 5: maps showing acid rain reduction in the Eastern United States](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2015-06/acid_rain.png)

*   A national system of marketable pollution allowances has dramatically cut power plant emissions of sulfur dioxide, reducing acid rain as well as secondary formation of fine particle pollution that contributes to premature death. [Acid rain](https://www.epa.gov/acidrain), which includes wet and dry deposition of acidic compounds from the atmosphere, results from emissions of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides.
*   Reducing acid rain has significantly reduced damage to water quality in lakes and streams, and improved the health of ecosystems and forests.
    *   Between the 1989 to 1991 and 2009 to 2011 observation periods, wet deposition of sulfate (which causes acidification) decreased by more than 55 percent on average across the eastern United States.
*   The dramatic emissions reductions achieved by the [acid rain program](https://www.epa.gov/acidrain/acid-rain-program) have helped to reduce atmospheric levels of fine particle pollution, avoiding numerous premature deaths.
*   Government and independent analyses have concluded that the benefits of the program far outweigh the costs, as detailed in the U.S. government's National Acid Precipitation Assessment Program (NAPAP) [2011 Report to Congress (pdf)](https://www.esrl.noaa.gov/csd/AQRS/reports/napapreport05.pdf) (132 pp, 14.5 MB).
*   Multiple analyses show that the cost of the fully implemented program is a fraction of the originally estimated cost - between $1-2 billion annually rather than the $6 billion EPA originally estimated in 1990, according to the NAPAP report.

### Interstate air pollution has been reduced.

Further reductions in power plant pollution have been achieved by state and EPA efforts to cut interstate air pollution, achieving additional public health benefits and helping downwind states meet health-based air quality standards for fine particles and ozone.

*   Twelve New England and mid-Atlantic states and the District of Columbia -- the Ozone Transport Region created by the 1990 Amendments -- worked together to create a nitrogen oxides (NOx) Budget Program and to adopt other controls that help improve ozone levels throughout the region.
*   Building on that success, EPA issued a broader [“NOx SIP Call”](https://www.epa.gov/power-sector/nox-budget-trading-program) Rule creating a similar NOx Budget Trading Program for much of the eastern United States, which ran from 2003 to 2008. As of 2008, the program cut summertime NOx emissions from power plants by 62 percent from 2000 levels. These reductions, along with the NOx reductions from federal motor vehicle standards, are responsible for substantial improvement in ozone levels across the eastern United States.
*   The subsequent Clean Air Interstate Rule (CAIR) has achieved large reductions in power plant annual SO2 and NOx emissions that contribute to fine particles, as well as some additional summertime NOx reductions beyond those required by the NOx SIP Call.
    *   CAIR, which had initial compliance deadlines in 2009 and 2010, is a major reason that almost all areas in the East have met the 1997 and 2006 air quality standards for fine particles. In the 2005 CAIR Regulatory Impact Analysis, EPA estimated that the reductions from the CAIR requirements would avoid 13,000 premature deaths a year in 2010.
*   CAIR was replaced by the [Cross-State Air Pollution Rule](https://www.epa.gov/Cross-State-Air-Pollution), as of January 1, 2015 to address the 1997 ozone National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS).
*   On September 7, 2016, the EPA revised the CSAPR by finalizing an update for the 2008 ozone NAAQS, known as the [CSAPR Update](https://www.epa.gov/Cross-State-Air-Pollution/final-cross-state-air-pollution-rule-update). CSAPR Update will further reduce summertime NOX emissions from power plants in the eastern U.S. and help downwind states to meet the new ozone standards.

### Mobile and industrial pollution sources release far less toxic air pollution than in 1990.

![Image 6: an industrial complex in Tacoma, WA 1972](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/2015-09/tacomasm.gif)

*   [Stationary sources](https://www.epa.gov/stationary-sources-air-pollution) today emit about 1.5 million tons less toxic air pollution per year than in 1990.[2](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#edn2)
    *   EPA has issued emissions standards to control toxic emissions from all 174 categories of major sources (e.g., chemical plants, oil refineries, aerospace manufacturing facilities, etc.), as well as from 68 categories of small “area” sources that represent 90 percent of the worst urban toxic pollutants.[3](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#edn3) States have elected to administer or enforce many of these federal standards.
*   Toxic emissions from onroad and nonroad vehicles and engines also are dropping due to requirements for cleaner fuels and engines.[4](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#edn4)
    *   These emissions are projected to be reduced by 80 percent by 2030 from 1990 levels.
    *   Onroad and nonroad diesel particulate matter emissions decreased by about 27 percent from 1990 to 2005 and are projected to be reduced an additional 90 percent from 2005 to 2030.
    *   Airborne levels of benzene, a carcinogen found in gasoline, declined by 66 percent from 1994 to 2009 based on available air quality monitoring information.[5](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#edn5)
*   Mercury emissions fell by about 80 percent between 1990 and 2014.[6](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/progress-cleaning-air-and-improving-peoples-health#edn6) EPA regulations for several large sources of mercury such as municipal waste combustion and medical waste incineration played a significant role.
*   Power plants remain the largest man-made source of mercury emissions in the United States, emitting more than half of all emissions of certain air toxics.
*   The 2012 [Mercury and Air Toxics Standards](https://www.epa.gov/mats) for power plants require power plants to reduce their emissions of mercury and other toxic air pollutants, protecting Americans from a host of avoidable illnesses and premature death.
    *   The pollutants reduced under MATS are associated with harm to the developing nervous systems of unborn babies and children, cancer, and with contributing to asthma and other respiratory diseases.
*   The compliance date for MATS was in 2015, and power plants have taken steps such as installing controls or updating operations to meet these standards that protect public health.
    *   The technologies used to reduce toxic pollution also reduce sulfur dioxide and fine particle pollution, with additional benefits for public health.
        *   MATS was estimated to prevent up to 11,000 premature deaths, 4,700 heart attacks and 130,000 asthma attacks annually beginning in 2016.
        *   The value of the quantified air quality improvements from MATS for people's health alone totals $37 billion to $90 billion each year. That means that for every dollar spent to reduce this pollution, Americans get $3-9 in health benefits. These significant health benefits do not include the benefits associated with reducing air toxics emitted from power plants because EPA does not at this time have the ability to quantify such benefits. Thus, the Agency is likely underestimating the benefits of the rule.
        *   The benefits of MATS are widely distributed and are especially important to minority and low income populations who are disproportionately impacted by asthma and other debilitating health conditions.
        *   Up to 540,000 missed work or "sick" days were estimated to be avoided each year beginning in 2016, enhancing productivity and lowering health care costs for American families.

### Actions to protect the ozone layer are saving millions of people from fatal skin cancers and eye cataracts.

![Image 7: map of ozone hole reduction in 2012](https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/styles/medium/public/2015-06/ozone_hole.png?itok=5zXEZVQ-)

*   [Actions to protect the stratospheric ozone layer](https://www.epa.gov/ozone-layer-protection) will save millions of American lives from skin cancer between 1990 and 2165. The actions also will avoid hundreds of millions of non-fatal skin cancers and tens of millions of cases of eye cataracts for Americans born between 1985 and 2100, according to a [peer-reviewed 1999 EPA study](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/benefits-and-costs-clean-air-act-1970-1990-study-design-and-summary-results).
*   The United States is one of 197 countries that are parties to the [Montreal Protocol](https://www.epa.gov/ozone-layer-protection/international-actions-montreal-protocol-substances-deplete-ozone-layer), an international treaty to protect the ozone layer. In 2012 the treaty marked its 25th anniversary. Helping developing countries comply through mechanisms like the Montreal Protocol's Multilateral Fund (MLF) will help assure success in restoring the ozone layer. Scientists estimate recovery by the middle of the 21st century. _[<Learn more about the Montreal Protocol.\>](https://www.epa.gov/ozone-layer-protection/international-treaties-and-cooperation-about-protection-stratospheric-ozone)_
*   Consistent with the Montreal Protocol, the Clean Air Act requires that EPA develop and implement regulations for the responsible management of ozone-depleting substances in the United States to help restore the ozone layer. The law uses multiple tools including the phase-out of certain chemicals, bans on nonessential products containing or made with such chemicals, and prohibition of the release of ozone-depleting refrigerants du

SUMMARY:
A control strategy related to air quality is a set of specific techniques and measures identified and implemented to achieve reductions in air pollution.

FULL CONTENT:
![U.S. flag](/themes/epa_theme/images/us_flag_small.png)

#### Breadcrumb

# Managing Air Quality - Control Strategies to Achieve Air Pollution Reduction

A control strategy related to air quality is a set of specific techniques and measures identified and implemented to achieve reductions in air pollution to attain an air quality standard or goal.

On this page:

## **Considerations in Designing an Effective Air Quality Control Strategy**

## **Controlling Sources of Pollution**

[Pollution prevention approaches](/p2) to reduce, eliminate, or prevent pollution at its source, should be considered. Examples are to use less toxic raw materials or fuels, use a less-polluting industrial process, and to improve the efficiency of the process.

[The Clean Air Technology Center](/catc) serves as a resource on air pollution prevention and control technologies, including their use, effectiveness and cost. Examples are mechanical collectors, wet scrubbers, fabric filters (baghouses), electrostatic precipitators, combustion systems (thermal oxidizers), condensers, absorbers, adsorbers, and biological degradation.

[Controlling emissions related to transportation](/transportation-air-pollution-and-climate-change/key-issues-websites-and-programs-related) can include emission controls on vehicles as well as use of cleaner fuels.

[Economic incentives](/environmental-economics/economic-incentives), such as emissions trading, banking, and emissions caps can be used. These strategies may be combined with the "command-and-control" type regulations which have traditionally been used by air pollution control agencies.

## **Need for Controls Applied Regionally or Nationally in Addition to Locally**

Air pollution does not recognize geographic boundaries.  Some pollutants can travel great distances affecting air quality and public health locally, regionally, nationally, and even internationally in areas that are downwind.

For this reason, control strategies to improve air quality in local areas need to include control measures that are mandated and implemented on a state, region-wide or national basis. In general, regulations established by the national government tend to have the widest application, which can minimize boundary and economic competition issues.

In the United States, the Clean Air Act requires that each state’s implementation plan contain provisions to prevent the emissions from the facilities or sources within its borders from contributing significantly to air quality problems in a downwind state. Learn more about the approach in the United States to address [interstate air pollution transport](/Cross-State-Air-Pollution/cross-state-air-pollution).

## **What are the Steps in Developing a Control Strategy?**

**​**Governments getting started in managing air quality should focus first on obvious sources of air pollution and the quickest means of controlling air emissions. More sophisticated and comprehensive strategies can be developed over time. The goal for all control strategies is to achieve real and measurable air emission reductions.

In the United States, control strategies to meet and maintain the national ambient air quality standards are developed by state governments. State governments adopt control measures through their legislative process and include them in state implementation plans, which need to be submitted and approved by EPA. The control measures are described and included in the plan.  Control measures that are part of an approved state implementation plan can be enforced by either the state or the national government.

Learn more regarding [basic information about air quality state implementation plans](/air-quality-implementation-plans/basic-information-about-air-quality-sips) and about [air quality implementation plans](/node/148797).

Learn more about how c[ost analysis models and tools for air pollution regulations](/economic-and-cost-analysis-air-pollution-regulations/cost-analysis-modelstools-air-pollution) support the assessment of emission reductions and engineering costs for air pollution control strategies.

[Learn more about reducing emissions of hazardous air pollutants](/haps/reducing-emissions-hazardous-air-pollutants) and a[ddressing stationary sources of air pollution](/stationary-sources-air-pollution) in the United States.

## [Air Quality Management Process](/air-quality-management-process)

# Air Pollution Solutions: 6 Pieces of Technology That Are Clearing Our Skies

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 3 2'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

### Newsletter

The development of new technology is one of the most promising frontiers for improving air quality. From catalytic converters to low-emitting consumer products, we’ve developed methods for preventing [air pollution](/en/air-academy/air-pollution) both indoors and outdoors.

In honor of these technological advancements, we are going to discuss six innovations that are actively preventing air pollutants from harming our atmosphere and our health. We’ve previously discussed [regulatory](/en/air-academy/air-pollution-solutions-public-policy) and [market-based solutions](/en/air-academy/air-pollution-solutions-market-policies) to air pollution, and both of these methods had their own respective pluses and minuses. Because new innovations don’t usually have the same kind of cost vs. benefit relationship as these other methods, instead of going through technology as an overarching solution, we are going to discuss these innovations individually.

## Multi-Pollutant Monitoring Devices

One of the topics we always love discussing is [air quality monitoring](/en/air-academy/air-quality-monitor-coverage); in this case, multi-pollutant air quality monitoring.

While this piece of technology does not directly reduce emissions or remove air pollution from our skies, it nevertheless is a crucial part of the air pollution control system. Pollution sources like factories, powerplants, and others emit more than one pollutant; on the flip side, some pollution control technologies control more than one pollutant. [Multi-pollutant monitoring](https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-management-process/managing-air-quality-multi-pollutant-planning-and-control) enables regulatory bodies to ensure compliance with emission limits for many different pollutants and reduces the cost and time burden associated with ambient pollution monitoring.

## Catalytic Converters

Vehicular exhaust is one of the most significant sources of air pollution globally. In response to increasingly strict environmental regulations beginning in the 1970s, gasoline- and diesel-powered vehicles were equipped with catalytic converters, a device that catalyzes a redox reaction that transforms dangerous air pollutants into less-harmful pollutants.

![traffic jam](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/traffic%20jam.jpg?quality=high&width=1051&name=traffic%20jam.jpg)

![traffic jam](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/traffic%20jam.jpg?quality=high&width=1051&name=traffic%20jam.jpg)

Gasoline-powered vehicles today generally come with a three-way converter, or an oxidation-reduction catalytic converter. These devices work by exposing exhaust to a [catalyst](http://www.cchem.berkeley.edu/molsim/teaching/fall2009/catalytic_converter/bkgcatcon.html), a substance that lowers the activation energy needed for a chemical reaction, thus enabling a reaction to convert [carbon monoxide](/en/air-academy/carbon-monoxide-co) to [carbon dioxide](/en/air-academy/carbon-dioxide-co2) and [nitrogen oxide](/en/air-academy/oxides-of-nitrogen-nox) to nitrogen and carbon dioxide. Carbon dioxide and atmospheric nitrogen are both far less harmful than carbon monoxide and nitrogen dioxide, despite CO2 being a GHG, so catalytic converters are great tools for reducing the environmental and health impacts of vehicular exhaust.

Catalytic converters do have some downsides. For one, these devices can restrict the flow of exhaust, reducing fuel efficiency and vehicle performance. Catalytic converters also have a warm-up period, during which the vehicle emits an unrestricted amount of air pollution. The creation of these converters also comes with some harms; many converters require platinum or palladium, rare metals that create pollution during the refining process.

## Scrubbers

Scrubbers are a type of pollution control device that removes air pollutants like [sulfur dioxide](/en/air-academy/sulfur-oxides-sox), chlorine, hydrogen sulfide, and hydrogen chloride from industrial exhaust.

![smokestack](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/smokestack.jpg?quality=high&width=1189&name=smokestack.jpg)

![smokestack](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/smokestack.jpg?quality=high&width=1189&name=smokestack.jpg)

There are two main types of scrubbers that we will discuss: wet scrubbers and dry scrubbers. Wet scrubbers use a liquid (usually water) to [absorb particles](https://www.epa.gov/air-emissions-monitoring-knowledge-base/monitoring-control-technique-wet-scrubber-particulate-matter) or [gases](https://www.epa.gov/air-emissions-monitoring-knowledge-base/monitoring-control-technique-wet-scrubber-gaseous-control) from a stream of air, and can vary in energy level. A common low energy wet scrubber is a spray tower, which works by passing the exhaust through an open vessel with sprayers to distribute the liquid. As the exhaust flows through the device, the liquid picks up floating particles or absorbs the target gas. Dry scrubbers work similarly, but instead of spraying a liquid, these devices spray dry reagents into the flue stream, neutralizing gases before they can enter the atmosphere.

Scrubbers are a hugely beneficial tool for pollution control, as they prevent damaging air pollutants from harming communities near industrial centers like powerplants and water treatment facilities. Additionally, these devices do not disrupt the production process, allowing economic and industrial activity to continue without a commensurate rise in air pollution.

## Chlorofluorocarbon (CFC) and Hydrochlorofluorocarbon (HCFC) Substitutes

The Montreal Protocol, signed in 1987, gradually phased out the production and use of chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and hydrofluorocarbons (HCFCs), both of which deplete the ozone layer. CFCs and HCFCs were commonly used as solvents, propellants, and refrigerants, so we needed to create alternatives that fulfilled the same purpose but with much lower ozone-depleting potential (ODP).

![air conditioning](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/air%20conditioning.jpg?quality=high&width=1187&name=air%20conditioning.jpg)

![air conditioning](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/air%20conditioning.jpg?quality=high&width=1187&name=air%20conditioning.jpg)

Today, hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) are one of the main substitutes for CFCs and HCFCs. The names can be a bit confusing, but, long story short, HFCs don’t have [chlorine](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120224110737.htm), which is the substance largely responsible for harming the ozone layer. The availability of HFCs and other substitutes is credited with the overwhelming success of the Montreal Protocol, as we were able to stop the production of CFCs and HCFCs without losing their respective functions.

However, HFCs are not harmless; while these substances don’t hurt the ozone layer, they do contribute to climate change. HFCs live much longer in the atmosphere than CFCs and HCFCs, which means that their impacts on global climate change are likewise long-term. The atmosphere is a very complicated system that’s constantly evolving, and the emissions that we produce need to likewise adapt. There are currently plans to expand the Montreal Protocol to include HFCs, as there are alternatives to these substances as well.

## Low-Emitting Stoves and Heaters

Air pollution is not just an outdoor issue; each year 3.8 [million people](https://www.who.int/en/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/household-air-pollution-and-health) die prematurely due to [indoor air pollution](/en/air-academy/indoor-air-quality) from inefficient cooking practices. Most of these deaths come from burning solid fuels or kerosene in enclosed spaces, allowing [particulate matter](/en/air-academy/particulate-matter-pm) levels to build up to hazardous levels.

[Innovations in stove design and alternative fuels](https://www.who.int/airpollution/household/interventions/technology/en/) have made cooking much more efficient and cut the amount of particulate matter produced in households. Biomass stoves can now come equipped with secondary combustions, fans, or insulated combustion chambers, which burn off unused fuel and ventilate the remaining pollutants. Ultimately we want to move away from biomass stoves, but these are good stepping stones for low- and middle-income nations where electricity or natural gas are not in steady supply.

![stove](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/stove.jpg?quality=high&width=1050&name=stove.jpg)

![stove](https://3782315.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/3782315/hubfs/stove.jpg?quality=high&width=1050&name=stove.jpg)

In addition to cooking, heating produces indoor air pollution. Solar water heaters and passive solar heating and cooling systems cut the necessity of solid fuel consumption, and improved ventilation designs, like stove hoods and chimneys, can help funnel out indoor air pollutants.

## VOC-Free Consumer Products and Building Materials

Lastly, we are going to discuss another innovation that reduces indoor air pollution: low-emitting consumer products and building materials.

Many of the products we use every day, including paints, cleaners, adhesives, sealants, furniture, and flooring off-gas [volatile organic compounds (VOCs)](/en/air-academy/understanding-tvoc-volatile-organic-compounds), which can build up without proper ventilation. Due to concerns surrounding the impacts of these emissions, more consumer products and building materials are being developed and certified as low-emitting or even [VOC-free](/en/air-academy/total-volatile-organic-compounds). [Guidelines](https://ehp.niehs.nih.gov/doi/10.1289/ehp.8988) offered by the California Department of Public Health and ASTM can be used to determine if a product is low-emitting.

Because these innovations are crucial for controlling indoor air pollution, many building standards require or offer credit for utilizing low-emitting building materials and including low-emitting consumer products as part of an indoor air quality strategy.

Many of the technologies we discussed above are big-picture, community-wide solutions. What can we, as individuals, do to protect ourselves from air pollution? For more information, check out our air pollution guide below:

[![Air Pollution: What You Need to Know](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/3782315/b1b977fe-749b-49a0-b63e-ed14d40aec76.png)](https://cta-redirect.hubspot.com/cta/redirect/3782315/b1b977fe-749b-49a0-b63e-ed14d40aec76)

![Air Pollution: What You Need to Know](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/3782315/b1b977fe-749b-49a0-b63e-ed14d40aec76.png)

Kaiterra provides [air quality monitors](https://www.kaiterra.com/) and an IAQ analytics dashboard for [healthy buildings](https://www.kaiterra.com/indoor-air-quality-monitor-for-buildings) and [offices](https://www.kaiterra.com/indoor-air-quality-monitoring-office), helping workplace leaders and healthy building pioneers assess and improve their indoor air quality. Our indoor air quality monitors like the [Sensedge](https://www.kaiterra.com/sensedge) and the [Sensedge Mini](https://www.kaiterra.com/sensedge-mini)can be found in many of the world’s most iconic buildings and workplaces, such as the Empire State Building and the Burj Khalifa.

--- SOURCE 5: The importance and challenges of air pollution control ---
URL: https://www.e3s-conferences.org/articles/e3sconf/pdf/2024/50/e3sconf_iceree2024_02003.pdf

SUMMARY:
by W Gongjun · 2024 · Cited by 1 — In conclusion, air pollution control is a key task for protecting human health, the environment, and sustainable development. The benefits of air pollution

FULL CONTENT:
a490009153@qq.com; b*Corresponding author’s email address: 403008168@qq.com The importance and challenges of air pollution control Wang Gongjun1,a, Wang Dongmei 2,b* 1Hubei three gorgespolytechnic, Yichang, Hubei, China 2JingzhouInstitute of Technology, Jingzhou, Hubei, China Abstract.Air pollution poses a serious threat to human health and the natural environment. Therefore, controlling air pollution is of vital importance. This article will explore the hazards of air pollution, the importance of air pollution control, the challenges faced, and possible solutions. At the same time, air pollution also has a significant impact on climate change. 1 Hazards of Air Pollution The annual exhaust gas emissions in China are approximately 650 million tons, of which 450 million tons are generated by coal-fired power generation, 150 million tons are from automobile exhaust emissions, and 50 million tons are from industrial exhaust gas emissions. The daily exhaust gas emissions in China are about 178,000 tons. It is reported that up to 7 million people die from air pollution every year. Air pollution caused by the use of fossil fuels can cause symptoms such as asthma, lung cancer, and stroke. 7 million people worldwide die from this every year, and about 60.65 billion yuan is spent on treating these diseases every year. Therefore, in order to address the problem of global warming and reduce the use of fossil fuels[1], it can not only improve the problem of air pollution, but also save many lives. Air pollution can cause various harms to human health, the environment, and the ecosystem, including but not limited to the following aspects: Health problems: Particulate matter and harmful gases in air pollution can enter the human respiratory system, causing health problems such as respiratory diseases, cardiovascular diseases, asthma, and lung cancer, especially being more severe for children, the elderly, and vulnerable groups[2]. Environmental damage: Air pollution causes damage to the natural environment, such as acid rain damaging plants and aquatic ecosystems and destroying soil quality. Climate change: Certain air pollutants, such as carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases, are one of the main factors causing global climate change. Economic impact: Air pollution may cause reduced crop yields, accelerated corrosion of buildings, increased energy consumption, and other economic losses. These damages highlight the importance of air pollution control. Taking effective measures to reduce air pollution is crucial for protecting human health, the environment, and the sustainable development of the ecosystem[3]. 2 The Importance of Air Pollution Control The importance of carrying out air pollution control is, first of all, an important measure to promote the construction of ecological civilization. Blue sky and white clouds are the most simple understanding of ecological civilization by the broad masses of the people. Without blue sky and white clouds, the comprehensive building of a moderately prosperous society, the construction of a beautiful China with ecological civilization, and the realization of the Chinese Dream of the great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation will be out of the question. It is necessary to use the breakthrough of comprehensive air pollution control to vigorously promote the construction of ecological civilization. Secondly, it is an inevitable requirement to solve the problems of the people's livelihood and the environment. An environment that does not harm the health of the people and is beautiful and livable is a public good and basic service that governments at all levels must provide[4]. By solidly promoting the comprehensive prevention and control of air pollution, significantly reducing heavy pollution weather, and gradually improving air quality, people can see hope and gain the trust of the people. Finally, it is a powerful grip to create a upgraded version of the Chinese economy. Our country's extensive development model of high input, high consumption, high pollution, and low efficiency has not been fundamentally changed, which is the fundamental cause of severe air pollution. Deepening the prevention and control of air pollution is the inherent requirement for changing the development mode and adjusting the economic structure, and it is an important measure to improve the quality and efficiency of economic growth. This is the statistics of the major air pollutants in our country from January to April 2022. E3S Web of Conferences 520, 02003 (2024) https://doi.org/10.1051/e3sconf/202452002003 ICEREE 2024 © The Authors, published by EDP Sciences. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). Fig 1. Statistics of Major Air Pollutant Concentrations in January-April 2022. (Derived from the official website of the Ministry of Ecological and Environmental Protection.) As show in figure 1. Lower PM2.5 concentrations usually indicate fewer fine particles in the air, which may be associated with better air quality. However, the relatively high concentration of PM10 may suggest that there is still a certain degree of particle pollution in the air. These particles may come from sources such as combustion, traffic emissions, and industrial activities[5]. High concentrations of ozone may have adverse effects on health, especially on the respiratory system. The concentration of ozone may be affected by meteorological conditions, industrial activities, and traffic emissions. Sulfur dioxide mainly comes from the combustion of fossil fuels and industrial processes. Although the concentration is low, it may still be related to certain industrial emissions or coal-burning activities[6]. Taken together, these data indicate the presence of a certain degree of pollutants in the air. 3 Challenges in Air Pollution Control Here are some challenges in air pollution control: Complex sources of pollution: Air pollution comes from a variety of sources, such as industrial waste gas, traffic emissions, coal-fired power plants, and agricultural activities. The complexity of pollution sources makes it difficult to identify the main sources of pollution and formulate effective control strategies[7]. Balance between economic development and environmental protection: In many areas, there is a contradiction between economic development and air pollution control. Some industries may be crucial to the economy, but they also produce a large amount of pollutants. Finding a balance between economic development and environmental protection is a challenge. Technological and cost limitations: Air pollution control requires advanced technologies and equipment, but the research and development and application of these technologies may be limited by costs. Some pollution control technologies may be expensive, posing an economic burden on businesses and governments. Energy structure adjustment: The energy structure in many areas is dominated by fossil fuels such as coal and oil. Reducing dependence on these energies and shifting to clean energy requires large-scale infrastructure renovation and policy support[8]. Cross-regional and global issues: Air pollution is not limited by geographical boundaries and may affect surrounding areas or even the entire planet. Solving cross-regional and global air pollution problems requires international cooperation and coordination. Public awareness and behavioral change: Raising public awareness of air pollution and prompting them to adopt more environmentally friendly lifestyles and consumption habits is an important aspect of air pollution control. Changing people's behaviors and concepts requires time and effective publicity and education. Implementation of policies and regulations: Formulating and implementing strict air quality standards and pollution emission regulations is crucial, but in practice, it may face difficulties in law enforcement, violations, and lack of effective supervision. Impact of climate change: Climate change may lead to changes in weather patterns, such as longer hot seasons and more frequent extreme weather events, which may exacerbate air pollution and affect the control effect[9]. Emergence of emerging pollutants: With the development of industry and technology, new pollutants may constantly emerge, posing new threats to air quality and human health. Monitoring and data quality: Accurate air quality monitoring and data analysis are essential for formulating effective control measures, but the coverage of the monitoring network and the accuracy of the data may have limitations. To overcome these challenges, comprehensive approaches are needed, including technological innovation, policy formulation, public education, and international cooperation. At the same time, continuous research and learning are also required to adapt to the changing environment and emerging issues. Only through global joint efforts can we effectively address the challenges of air pollution control, improve air quality, and protect human health and the environment[10]. 4 Solutions to Air Pollution Control Air pollution poses a serious threat to human health and the natural environment, becoming one of the significant challenges faced globally. With the rapid development of industrialization and urbanization, the problem of air pollution has become increasingly acute. Therefore, it is crucial to adopt effective measures to control air pollution. Automobile exhaust is one of the main sources of urban air pollution. To reduce automobile exhaust emissions, we can encourage the use of public transportation, walking, or cycling, and other green travel methods. For example, many cities have established bike-sharing systems, providing convenient bike rental services, and encouraging people to choose environmentally friendly travel methods. In addition, promoting the use of electric and new energy vehicles is also an important measure to reduce exhaust pollution. Industrial emissions are another important source of air pollution. Factories and enterprises should take responsibility for reducing pollution. For example, some factories have adopted advanced pollution control equipment, such as flue gas desulfurization and denitrification devices, effectively reducing the emissions Statistics of Major Air pollutant Concentrations in January-April 2022 49 79 119 13 30 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140 PM2.5 PM10 O3 SO2 NO2 concentration ug/m3 AQI 2 E3S Web of Conferences 520, 02003 (2024) https://doi.org/10.1051/e3sconf/202452002003 ICEREE 2024 of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides. In addition, the government can strengthen environmental supervision, formulate stricter emission standards, and impose severe penalties for illegal emissions. In addition to transportation and industry, some behaviors in daily life can also lead to air pollution. For example, burning garbage and straw will release a large amount of particulate matter and harmful gases. To reduce this pollution, we can advocate waste sorting and recycling to reduce waste generation. At the same time, farmers can adopt more environmentally friendly ways to handle straw, such as returning straw to the field or making it into organic fertilizers. Air pollution control requires the joint efforts of the government, enterprises, and the public. The government can formulate and implement relevant laws and regulations, strengthen environmental monitoring and supervision. Enterprises should actively take environmental protection measures to reduce pollution emissions. The public can contribute to air pollution control by changing their lifestyles and choosing environmentally friendly products and services[11]. The emission of air pollutants is generally calculated using the formula: E = Q × C × T × 10-9 (1) E is the actual emission of pollutants in the air, tons; Q is the flue gas flow rate, m3/h; C is the emission concentration of the pollutant, mg/m3. T is the emission time of the pollutant, hours. 5 The impact of air pollution on climate change The impact of air pollution on climate change is an issue that is receiving increasing attention. Air pollutants caused by human activities, such as carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide and other greenhouse gases, as well as aerosols, not only pose a threat to air quality and human health, but also have a significant impact on the global climate[12]. Firstly, greenhouse gases in air pollution contribute to climate warming. These gases can block the heat released from the Earth's surface, causing the temperature of the Earth's surface to rise. The most significant greenhouse gas is carbon dioxide, and its increasing concentration is one of the main causes of global climate warming. Since the industrial era, human beings have emitted a large amount of carbon dioxide through the use of fossil fuels, resulting in a sharp increase in the concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. This has triggered a series of phenomena of climate change, such as the rise in the global average temperature, the melting of glaciers, and the rise in sea levels[13]. Secondly, aerosols in air pollution also have an impact on climate change. Aerosols refer to tiny particles suspended in the air, including dust, smoke, sulfates, nitrates, etc. Aerosols can scatter and absorb solar radiation, thereby affecting the Earth's energy balance. On the one hand, aerosols can reduce the solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface by reflecting sunlight, resulting in a decrease in ground temperature, which is called the cooling effect of aerosols. On the other hand, some aerosols can also absorb long-wave radiation emitted from the Earth's surface, resulting in a warming of the atmosphere, which is called the heating effect of aerosols. The impact of aerosols on the climate depends on their type, concentration, and distribution, as well as other factors, so the overall impact of aerosols on climate change is complex. The impact of sulfate aerosols on the climate. Sulfate aerosols mainly come from coal-fired and industrial activities, as well as natural sources such as volcanic eruptions. Sulfate aerosols can reflect solar radiation, thereby reducing the temperature of the Earth's surface. However, when sulfate aerosols combine with water vapor to form clouds, they can change the properties and lifespan of the clouds, thereby affecting the reflection and absorption of solar radiation by the clouds. This may lead to a decrease in the reflection ability of the clouds, allowing more solar radiation to reach the Earth's surface, thereby exacerbating climate warming[14]. In addition, air pollution can indirectly affect the climate by affecting the atmospheric circulation and water cycle. For example, the presence of aerosols can affect the formation and properties of clouds, thereby changing the absorption and reflection of solar radiation. This may lead to changes in cloud cover, changes in precipitation patterns, and other aspects of the climate system. To address the challenges of air pollution and climate change, the international community is taking a series of measures, including reducing greenhouse gas emissions, improving the energy structure, increasing energy efficiency, and promoting clean transportation. At the same time, individuals can also contribute to reducing air pollution and addressing climate change by changing their lifestyles, such as reducing energy consumption, using public transportation, and encouraging low-carbon travel. 6 Conclusion In conclusion, air pollution control is a key task for protecting human health, the environment, and sustainable development. The benefits of air pollution control include[15]: Reducing the emissions of pollutants makes the air fresher, which is beneficial to people's respiratory health. Air pollution control helps to protect the living environment of plants, animals, and other organisms, and maintains ecological balance. Reducing air pollution can reduce the occurrence of health problems related to pollution, such as respiratory and cardiovascular diseases. Good air quality can improve people's quality of life and create a more comfortable living and working environment. Controlling air pollution contributes to the rational use of resources and the sustainable development of the environment. We can take the following measures: Formulate and implement stricter pollution emission standards, forcing enterprises to adopt clean production technologies. 3 E3S Web of Conferences 520, 02003 (2024) https://doi.org/10.1051/e3sconf/202452002003 ICEREE 2024 Increase the use of renewable and clean energy and reduce dependence on fossil fuels. Encourage the use of public transportation and promote electric vehicles to reduce vehicle exhaust emissions. Require industrial enterprises to install pollution control equipment, improve production processes, and reduce waste gas emissions. Strengthen the monitoring and law enforcement of pollution emissions, and severely punish violations. Through education and publicity activities, raise public awareness of air pollution issues and prompt them to take environmental protection actions. Respond to transboundary air pollution problems through international cooperation, and share experiences and technologies. The comprehensive implementation of these measures can effectively control air pollution, bringing cleaner air and a healthier environment. At the same time, air pollution control is a long-term and continuous process that requires the joint efforts and active participation of all parties. References 1. enriched_points.expanded_content: Control technologies are crucial for reducing harmful emissions, improving air quality, and protecting public health, thereby supporting sustainable environmental practices.
Air pollution control technologies can be categorized into regulatory measures, monitoring systems, and emission reduction technologies, each serving vital roles in pollution management.
These technologies function by capturing, neutralizing, or eliminating pollutants through various means such as filtration, absorption, and chemical reactions, ensuring cleaner air.
